topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the otherwise unremarkable flow of my afternoon. It was an alert: '14 Willow Creek Lane - Now Available.' I stared at the listing for a full minute, my gaze drifting from the glossy photos of peeling paint and overgrown gardens to the stark price tag. A faint sigh escaped my lips, barely audible over the hum of the air conditioning. I picked up my lukewarm coffee, the ceramic mug feeling heavy and inert in my hand. I scrolled past the details, my thumb moving mechanically over the screen, registering the information without any real spark of feeling. It was just data, a transaction waiting to happen, completely separate from my current reality. I set the phone down without saving anything, the weight of the potential move settling on me like a damp cloth.

[story 2]

I was cleaning out my inbox, a chore I usually approached with a dull resignation, when the email landed. It was an old address, a place I hadn't consciously thought about in years, and now it was up for auction. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning in protest. There was no surge of nostalgia, no sudden ache. Instead, a strange flatness settled over my chest, a kind of emotional anesthetic. I tapped the screen to open the listing. The house looked exactly as I remembered it—dusty, familiar, utterly unchanged by my absence. I traced the outline of the front porch with my finger, feeling nothing more than the slight texture of the glass. "Well," I murmured to the empty room, my voice flat, "that's that." Then, I closed the browser tab and moved on to checking my schedule.

[story 3]

The listing popped up during a mindless web search, and I paused, my scrolling hand freezing mid-air. It was the address of my childhood residence. I blinked slowly, processing the information as if it were a piece of unrelated trivia. I didn't feel the usual pull—no sudden longing or sharp memory flashes. Instead, a sort of detachment washed over me, making my shoulders drop slightly. I glanced at the picture of the dilapidated garden, and a neutral assessment crossed my mind: 'Old. Needs work.' I didn't click on the details. I simply let the notification sit there, a digital ghost, and returned to the task at hand, the weight of the discovery dissolving into the background noise of my everyday routine.
